RE: Learning JS from Zero — Day 5: Arrays and Objects
Day 5 of getting reincarnated into JavaScript. This time we're going to figure out what arrays and objects are.

You can think of an array as a really special kind of variable. Normally a variable can only hold one thing — a string, a number, a boolean. Picture a variable as a box that fits exactly one item. An array is different. Think of it like a storage skill in one of those isekai anime — it can hold a whole bunch of stuff at once.
Normally when we declare a variable we just write
. But say we needed to store the numbers 1 through 5. Without arrays, that's five separate variables:
The nice thing about an array is that you only declare one variable and it can hold all of 1 through 5.
There are two ways to create an array. The first is the constructor form,
. I personally don't recommend that one. Instead I'd go with the way most people use day to day — the so-called
(also called array literal syntax). The big win with array literals is readability, so that's the one I'll focus on here. Here's what an array literal looks like:
One more thing — in some programming languages an array can
only hold values of the same type
, meaning the whole array is either all strings or all numbers. But because of how JavaScript works, you can mix any types you want inside one array. So this is totally fine:
Getting a value back out of an array is easy too. Arrays start counting from 0, so you just write the variable name plus square brackets with a number inside:

var arrayData = ['this is a string', 10, true, null, undefined];
arrayData[0]; // gives you 'this is a string'
So reading from an array looks like this:
Besides reading, you can also directly overwrite the value at a specific position. For example, changing the slot that was
// now you get 'this is an array'
So from all of that we can see an array can hold any data type (including objects and functions). That's why an array is sometimes described as

Objects and arrays are closely related. In real projects you'll constantly see objects inside arrays, and arrays inside objects. You can build an object with the constructor
— but again, not many people use it in practice, myself included. Most real-world code uses the
(also called object literal syntax), for the same reason as arrays: better readability.

Earlier we pictured an array as a storage skill. So what's an object? You can also think of an object as a storage skill — but the special part is that it's a storage skill with labels for sorting things. There's really only one key idea with objects:
an object is a pairing of names and values
. What does that mean? Let's look at some real examples.
First, here's how you create an object literal:
So how do you read a value out of an object? There are two main ways. The first is the dot operator:
The second is bracket notation. With brackets, the name inside has to be wrapped in single or double quotes:
And you can change an object's value using either of those two ways:
One concept I want to stress again:
an object is just a pairing of names and values
. You can honestly think of it as another way to declare variables — which is why in the image I labeled it
JavaScript Itself Is Just One Big Object
Once you understand objects and arrays, you start to notice something. In the past few posts we've been using the dot operator to access the features JavaScript gives us. But have you ever wondered —
could we use bracket notation to call those features instead?
For example, when we debug we constantly reach for
to track down problems. What if we switched to brackets? Do you think it'd work? Let's look at a real example:
So we can see JavaScript itself is basically one giant pile of objects. I think if you go look at
now, it'll click a lot harder:
In real development the dot operator is just more convenient for calling features — one fewer key to press, after all. One last thing to add about the dot operator: in objects, its proper name is the
, and the "member" refers to the name inside the object. We just call it the dot operator most of the time because it reads more intuitively.
(Thanks to Huli for pointing out a spot I got wrong XD)
The next post, RE: Learning JS from Zero — Day 6, will cover operators.

Day 5 of my reincarnation into JavaScript. This time we get into arrays and objects — two of the most-used data structures in JS. We'll cover array literals, reading and changing values, object literals, dot notation vs bracket notation, and why JavaScript itself is basically just one giant object.
